熱刺是一間座落英國倫敦北部托定咸區的職業足球會
從1882年成立至今已經擁有過百年歷史，現時競逐於英格蘭超級聯賽；
球會主場為熱刺球場，球會的拉丁文格言 ”Audere est Facere (To Dare Is To Do)” 則意謂「敢作敢為」

The days of Mauricio Pochettino's snarling, aggressive yet creative Tottenham Hotspur side that challenged for the Premier League title two years in a row are long gone.

They began to dissolve during the Argentine's reign and have completely vanished now under his successor Jose Mourinho.

I asked Pochettino towards the end of his reign where the collective hassling, harrying and pressing, the hallmarks that had become so identified with his young, hungry side at their best had gone.

He just didn't seem to know, admitting that he wanted more desire and energy from his players but wasn't getting it.

It was a rot that had set in back then - with the club failing to push on and evolve from those two promising seasons - and has now spread throughout.

The attackers were a collection of individuals who appeared to vaguely know each other from somewhere but had no idea what they were meant to be doing.

There is so much work to do for Mourinho and while he has made strides with the defence, the attack is just so disjointed and seems to lack any plan.

"Performance not good enough, but good enough to win," Mourinho said after the game. "I am not saying a strong performance, I'm not saying a sharp performance but I say good enough to win.

"In a way they surprised us because of the way they played because since I remember Bournemouth in the Premier League I don't remember them playing the way they did, but was an option and result for them as they have a point they wanted.

"They played very direct, only direct. They didn't move the ball in midfield in the first phase like they used to do and they made it more difficult for us with their approach."

For Mourinho, one of the game's renowned tacticians and with the attacking riches he has and has admitted his delight at having in recent weeks, to blame the opposition's set-up was weak.

"For a long time we were slow in our actions, we didn't have sharpness in attacking area," he added.

"I made changes that I think improved the team. The team in the last 15-20 minutes was the period where we were more dominant, but again the game stopped too many times."

Spurs have gone from suddenly scoring goals under Mourinho but conceding plenty, to shutting up shop at the back and looking like they have to rely on own goals or mistakes at the other end.

Harry Kane seems to be wondering where his next pass or cross will come from and even Giovani Lo Celso looked tired and frustrated whenever he raised his heard and saw no movement from the attacking midfielders.

Mourinho has predicted on a couple of occasions during his tenure that Tottenham will challenge for the Premier League title next season.

He needs more than just a few tweaks here and there to make that happen. He needs to give the players a plan and belief again and if they are not responding then get them out of there.

Mourinho needs to cut out the excuses. They only add to the 'woe is me' narrative for his players that they're put upon.

The 'them and us' scenario worked at his other clubs because Mourinho had built a team that thrived on that mentality.

Tottenham, in its current state, doesn't work like that. They get carried away with the highs and consumed by the lows.

The Champions League final is the biggest example of the latter. What should have been the greatest moment in the club's history is still weighing like an anchor around the moping club's neck.

Mourinho has the experience and the CV to show that he can transform the mentality at a club.

He told football.london that he needs time to do it and his chairman Daniel Levy is going to have a crucial role to play this summer in learning from past mistakes when the team desperately needed to push on and failed to.


The failures to push on on the pitch in recent years have come from every level of the club, starting at the top and ending in the players out there on the turf.

Sunday's north London derby, on paper, should be the most meaningless of all its predecessors but it could provide a watershed moment for Mourinho - either the day when a clear way forward was shown or when the sheer size of the task ahead was revealed.

Speaking in his pre-match press conference ahead of the weekend’s north London derby, Mourinho has explained his reasoning behind bringing the Frenchman into the action

"I had Giovani with a problem, he was kicked in the ankle. He was not feeling comfortable. He was also coming from 90 minutes very hard against Everton,” he said.

"The feeling was we are going to have the ball, going to have space to play, not space to penetrate but space to play, to have the ball and with the midfield players I had on the bench: Skipp, Gedson, the kid Harvey, Tanguy I felt he was the one with that talent to move the ball.

"I think he was the same level as the team, not better and not worse."

Ndombele will be hoping to be given another chance to impress when Spurs take on Arsenal on Sunday.

“I’m worried about Tottenham,” Crouch said in response to a reader’s question to his Daily Mail column.

“I look at the body language of the players and it isn’t great. I see people struggling like Dele Alli, who had been so influential for Mauricio Pochettino, and Tanguy Ndombele, who arrived with such a big reputation, and it concerns me.

“The first area I would look to improve is at full back. I believe they can improve on Serge Aurier at right back, for certain.

“The challenge of finding a long-term partnership to replace Jan Vertonghen and Toby Alderweireld will not be easy but it’s imperative that they find answers.

“When you look at Tottenham’s squad it really isn’t far away, and with the right additions I believe they can get back to challenging for a top-four spot.

“It just hasn’t gone anywhere near how everyone expected it would with Jose Mourinho. The fizz is badly missing.”

The Special One certainly has his work cut out if he wants to make the Lilywhites a force in both the Premier League and Europe again.
